next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

Once he'd decided to risk showing someone one page, he spent three days choosing which page to bring.

The manuscript had fifty-seven sheets — he had counted them twice, turning each one by the edges with his thumbs clear of the notation because even now he still half expected the material to react if he handled it like an ordinary page. Most of the sheets were dense with notation that moved when he looked at it, relationships between symbols shifting like tidal patterns he could not predict. But a few pages were sparser. One near the middle had what looked like paired entries: a symbol cluster on the left, a simpler arrangement on the right. If any page was a key, this was it.

He folded it carefully — the material was not paper and did not crease easily, taking the fold with a faint resistance, like bending something that was used to being flat — and slid it into his Ancient Runes textbook between the chapter on Elder Futhark variations and the one he had not read yet.

Ancient Runes was the last class on Wednesdays. The classroom was on the third floor, a small room that still smelled like a school — ink and old binding glue and the specific warmth of reference books that had been absorbing body heat from generations of students leaning over them. Professor Babbling had not changed the room this year. The shelves were still lined with comparative notation charts and stone fragment replicas and the framed rubbing of the Rök runestone that Babbling touched sometimes when she passed it, an absent, habitual gesture, like checking a doorknob.

The class was small...
open_hooks_to_carry: The sub-collection rooms must feel genuinely old and other — cut stone, no torches, light from the walls themselves, limestone-cold air
The manuscript must be rendered as physically strange: not parchment, slightly warm, notation that moves representationally
prev_tail_excerpt:
...attress any other way.

His shoulders and arms pulsed. His knees felt like they'd been worked over with hammers. His palms stuck to the sheet where the half-moon cuts had dried tacky.

He thought about the card: *Sub-Collection C. Pre-Foundation Acquisitions,* the little shelf number in dust-brown ink, the title he couldn't read, the description in old brown ink: *a teaching instrument concerning the substrate upon which all secondary magical structures depend.* The location directions that assumed you could identify a room by the temperature of its air or the sound a wall made.

He had found it. Not the manuscript itself, but where it was. And he'd found it not because he was clever or skilled or brave — he'd found it because Carrow had beaten him for three hours and the staircase had moved and he'd been too tired and hurt to go back the right way.

His right knee was definitely bleeding. He could feel it, a warm stickiness inside his trouser leg that was different from the rest of the pain, more ordinary, more familiar. Like a skinned knee from falling off a bicycle, except he hadn't fallen off anything. He'd been kneeling.

He closed his eyes. The room was dark and his roommates were breathing and tomorrow he had Potions and his knees were ruined and his back was a map of hex-burns and somewhere up on the seventh floor, past the narrow place and the room of empty shelves and the stretch where the stone changed color, there was a manuscript that Bathilda Bagshot had dismissed in a footnote and Madam Pince had tried to push him away from.

He fell asleep with his shoes on.
state_deltas_required: The strangeness of the manuscript itself — it resists comprehension, its materials are alien, its notation moves in ways that are not wizarding-photograph movement but something representational and unfamiliar
Gavin has the manuscript but cannot read it. Obsession begins as he spends weeks failing to decode notation that maps to no known system
